extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Thermal incubator system comprising an insulated styrofoam container (0.03 m³ volume) with a heatbed (214×214×3 mm), fan (0.02 m³/s airflow), temperature sensors (DS18S20, -55°C to +125°C, 0.5°C accuracy), and Raspberry Pi 4 Model B controller. The system regulates temperature within the box (20°C to ~26.85°C) by heating air (0.04 kg mass, 700 J/kg·K specific heat) and distributing it via a fan. It serves as a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) with physical (insulated container, heatbed, fan) and cyber components (controller, sensors, communication).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Monitoring (real-time temperature tracking via sensors), control (Raspberry Pi-based temperature regulation), prediction (simulation models for thermal inertia analysis), optimization (calibration algorithms for model accuracy), and visualization (data sharing via RabbitMQ for DT synchronization). Services target engineers and system operators for system performance analysis, fault detection, and operational decision-making.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (GPIO for sensor/fan control), DS18S20 temperature sensors, RabbitMQ (message broker for communication server), optocouplers/MOSFETs (signal isolation for 4A current handling), PCB design (component connectivity), simulation models (2-parameter and 4-parameter thermal models), and open-source repository (GitHub: https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) for hardware/software access.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time visualization of incubator status is achieved through continuous data relay from the controller and low-level driver to a time series database. Calibration experiments use historical data trajectories, and what-if simulations relay real-time or historical data from the Physical Twin (PT). The Kalman filter for heatbed state estimation operates in real-time to detect anomalies. RabbitMQ enables synchronized communication between the controller and low-level driver with low-latency message passing.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Two simulation models (Model A and Model B) are calibrated with different parameter sets (Model A: 2 parameters, Model B: 4 parameters). The self-adaptation loop involves multiple instances of calibration, anomaly detection, and what-if simulations. The system supports multiple temperature sensors (3 deployed) and hierarchical coordination between the controller, communication server, and models.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin (DT) framework integrates a communication server (RabbitMQ), low-level drivers, and models (Model A/B) for calibration and simulation. Calibration algorithms update models with parameter estimates, and monitors use data to check system safety. The self-adaptation loop closes the DT loop by re-configuring the controller based on new experiments. The DT orchestrates data flow, model updates, and control logic through the Raspberry Pi and 1-Wire bus.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The system uses RabbitMQ (open-source message broker) for interoperability between components. The open-source hardware/software repository (GitHub) allows integration with external systems. The DT communicates with the Physical Twin (PT) via sensors and actuators, and data exchange follows the 1-Wire protocol for temperature sensors. The DT can interface with enterprise systems through the communication server and time series database.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin models include behavioral and analytical models (e.g., differential equations for dynamics, thermal inertia prediction). Data sources are sensors on the Physical Twin (PT) with time-series collection. Models integrate cyber-physical aspects (e.g., heatbed thermal properties). Calibration algorithms update models with parameters like C_air (J/K), G_box (J/K), C_heater (J/K), G_heater (J/K). Models are validated against experimental data trajectories. Data management involves time-series databases for real-time visualization and co-simulations. Model B (four parameters) provides higher fidelity for heatbed inertia prediction.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators include a heatbed (controlled via MOSFETs for 4A current) and a fan. Control mechanisms use Raspberry Pi GPIO with optocouplers to decouple signals. Actuation ranges: heatbed power regulation (via voltage control), fan speed modulation. Remote interfaces: GPIO pins and RabbitMQ for command transmission. Safety constraints: overcurrent protection via MOSFETs, thermal limits to prevent overheating. Addressing: unique 64-bit serial codes for sensors, direct GPIO mapping for actuators.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors: DS18S20 1-Wire digital thermometers (accuracy ±0.5°C from -10°C to +85°C, range -55°C to +125°C). Three sensors deployed spatially. Sampling frequency: implicit in experimental data collection (not explicitly stated). Data transmission: 1-Wire bus with serial codes enabling multi-sensor operation. Data collection mechanisms: low-level drivers abstracting sensor communication, integrating with RabbitMQ for real-time data streaming.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity requires parameter calibration (e.g., C_air=486.12 J/K for Model B) via nonlinear least squares optimization using Scipy. Validation methods: residual error minimization (targeting <threshold), comparison with experimental data trajectories. Uncertainty sources: sensor inaccuracies (±0.5°C), model parameter variations. Error detection: Kalman filter for heatbed state estimation. Quality assurance: iterative calibration, self-adaptation loops updating models post-experiment, and co-simulation with real-time PT data.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical sensors (DS18S20 temperature sensors) to the digital twin via RabbitMQ. Data types include temperature readings (-55°C to +125°C, 0.5°C accuracy). Communication protocol: RabbitMQ (MQTT-like message broker). Transmission frequency: Real-time updates via continuous monitoring. Event triggers: Temperature deviations detected by sensors. Data quality assurance: Sensor calibration and filtering. Preprocessing steps: Data aggregation and normalization before transmission. Data integration mechanisms: RabbitMQ message queues for asynchronous data delivery.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands (fan speed, heatbed power) are sent from the digital twin to physical components via RabbitMQ. Command types: Setpoint adjustments for temperature regulation. Control message formats: JSON-based payloads with temperature targets. Validation/authentication: Command parameter checks (e.g., within safe voltage ranges). Feedback systems: Real-time temperature sensor data to validate control actions. Conflict resolution: Prioritizes safety constraints over user commands. Command execution confirmation: Acknowledgment via RabbitMQ message delivery receipts.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication protocols: RabbitMQ (AMQP 0-9-1) for asynchronous message passing. Network topology: Star architecture (Raspberry Pi as central hub). Bandwidth requirements: Low-latency (<100ms) for real-time control. Latency requirements: <500ms for temperature regulation. Reliability mechanisms: Message persistence and retries in RabbitMQ. Redundancy: Dual temperature sensors for fault tolerance. Security protocols: TLS encryption for data in transit (RabbitMQ configuration).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Hosting platforms: Edge (Raspberry Pi 4 Model B) and cloud (RabbitMQ server). Computational requirements: Raspberry Pi 4 (4-core Cortex-A53, 1.5GHz). Storage requirements: Time-series database for logged sensor data. Scalability: Modular architecture allows adding sensors/actuators. Deployment models: Containerized microservices (Docker). Availability assurance: Redundant power supply and watchdog timers. Reliability assurance: Fail-safe shutdown protocols for hardware failures.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle includes development stages: initial model creation (two-parameter and four-parameter simulation models), calibration experiments (Model A and B parameterization), and deployment phases with real-time monitoring. Lifecycle governance involves continuous calibration, model updates (e.g., recalibrating Model B when new objects like cold water are introduced), and self-adaptation loops. Representation types transition from simulation models to operational models integrated with physical systems.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering processes include model calibration (e.g., C_air=616.56 J/K, G_box=0.65 J/K for Model A; C_heater=33.65 J/K, G_heater=0.87 J/K for Model B), version control via open-source repositories, and evolution strategies like recalibrating models and reconfiguring Kalman filters. DT evolution involves co-simulations for what-if scenarios, automated calibration triggers, and integrating feedback loops (e.g., anomaly detection to schedule experiments).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support includes real-time visualization of temperature data via dashboards, anomaly detection using Kalman filters, and heatbed state estimation. Analytics capabilities involve predictive modeling of thermal inertia (Model B) and optimization of controller parameters. User interaction includes automated calibration processes and simulation-based scenario testing. Confidence measures are derived from model calibration accuracy and Kalman filter residuals.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with open-source frameworks (RabbitMQ, GitHub), 1-Wire digital thermometer protocols, and CPS standards. Interoperability is achieved through MQTT/AMQP messaging (RabbitMQ), 1-Wire bus for sensors, and modular hardware/software design (e.g., PCB decoupling with optocouplers). No specific industry standards are explicitly cited, but adherence to open-source and CPS best practices is emphasized.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **6 Summary**


We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

specifications of the case study.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **6 Summary**


We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,

Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:

**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and

more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature

develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

**3.3** **Calibration**


Although the dynamics models have been built, they contain free parameters. In order
to utilise the models, the models need to be calibrated for the incubator. The results of
the calibration are detailed in section 4.2, and here we detail the procedure.
A non-linear least squares solver was used to calibrate the parameters of the models
derived above. Then the models are evaluated using the estimated parameters. Afterwards the resulting behaviour is compared with a data trajectory that is obtained by
running an experiment of the incubator. Finally using an optimisation function inside
the python Scipy package [14] to obtain the values of the parameters. Such a function
finds the parameters that minimizes the residual error. When the residual error is small
enough, the optimised parameters can be obtained.
The principle of the package is based on least-square, it assumes that:


_y_ = _f_ ( _x, θ_ ) + _ϵ_ (10)


where _f_ ( _x, θ_ ) is the model needed to be calibrated, _θ_ are the parameters represented in
a vector in the model, and _y_ is the label or the measured data.
A cost function or an objective function can be provided as:


_J_ = �( _y_ _i_ _−_ _f_ ( _x_ _i_ _, θ_ )) [2] (11)


The objective is to acquire a minimal value of _J_, and at the same time, the corresponding _θ_ are the value needed. In order to minimise the cost function, the most commonly
used one is gradient descent. After multiple iterations, the value of the cost function
goes to a sufficient small value and the _θ_ are obtained simultaneously.

The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **6 Summary**


We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.
